policy.title: RBI Issues Final Guidelines on Climate Risk Management for Scheduled Commercial Banks
policy.description: RBI mandated all scheduled commercial banks to integrate climate scenario analysis into ICAAP by March 2027, with physical risk stress testing required for top 50 borrowers by exposure. Banks must report climate-adjusted PD/LGD metrics quarterly starting FY2027-28.

policy.title: SEBI Expands BRSR Core to Include Climate Transition Plans for Top 500 Companies
policy.description: SEBI's updated BRSR Core framework now requires top 500 listed companies by market cap to disclose 1.5°C-aligned transition plans and Scope 3 emissions by FY2027. Reasonable assurance on climate metrics becomes mandatory from FY2028.
policy.source: Securities and Exchange Board of India

policy.title: IRDAI Releases Sandbox Guidelines for Index-Based Climate Insurance Products
policy.description: IRDAI opened a regulatory sandbox for parametric insurance products covering flood, cyclone, and heat stress triggers with a 45-day payout requirement. Insurers can now partner with banks for embedded parametric covers on agricultural and MSME loan portfolios.
policy.source: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India

policy.title: Basel Committee Finalizes Pillar 3 Climate Risk Disclosure Requirements
policy.description: BCBS published final Pillar 3 disclosure standards requiring banks to report climate-related credit risk concentrations by sector and geography from January 2028. Physical risk exposure metrics must include 30-year forward-looking hazard assessments.

policy.title: IFRS Foundation Announces 15 Jurisdictions Committed to Mandatory ISSB Adoption by 2027
policy.description: India confirmed adoption of IFRS S1 and S2 standards for large corporates beginning April 2027, with the Ministry of Corporate Affairs working on convergence with existing BRSR requirements. This aligns India with Singapore, Hong Kong, and the UK adoption timeline.

policy.title: NGFS Releases Phase V Climate Scenarios with Enhanced Physical Risk Pathways
policy.description: Updated NGFS scenarios include granular South Asian physical risk projections with district-level acute hazard frequencies for India. The scenarios incorporate monsoon variability and compound heat-flood events calibrated to 2025 climate observations.
policy.source: Network for Greening the Financial System

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence Partners with HDFC Bank for Physical Risk Assessment of Commercial Loan Book
competitors.description: Jupiter signed a multi-year deal to provide asset-level flood and cyclone risk scores for HDFC Bank's ₹8 lakh crore commercial real estate and infrastructure portfolio. This marks Jupiter's first major Indian banking client.

competitors.title: Sprih Raises $12M Series A to Expand Climate Risk Modules for Indian Banks
competitors.description: Bengaluru-based Sprih closed a Series A led by Elevar Equity to build physical risk assessment tools targeting mid-sized NBFCs. The company claims 40+ enterprise clients including three public sector banks.

competitors.title: MSCI Launches India-Specific Climate Value-at-Risk Module with RBI Scenario Alignment
competitors.description: MSCI released a localized Climate VaR product calibrated to RBI's climate stress testing requirements, covering 1,200 Indian listed companies and 500 unlisted borrowers. Pricing starts at $150,000 annually for bank licenses.

competitors.title: XDI Expands Cross Dependency Initiative Database to Cover 50 Million Indian Assets
competitors.description: Australian climate risk firm XDI added granular physical risk scores for Indian industrial facilities, warehouses, and residential properties at PIN code level. The dataset integrates cyclone, flood, and heat stress projections to 2100.

competitors.title: Moody's Partners with CRISIL to Integrate India Credit and Climate Data
competitors.description: Moody's and CRISIL announced a joint platform combining Moody's physical risk models with CRISIL's Indian corporate credit ratings. The product targets Indian banks seeking integrated PD adjustment factors for climate risk.

competitors.title: Enture Launches Automated BRSR Core Reporting Platform with Climate Module
competitors.description: Mumbai-based Enture released an end-to-end BRSR compliance tool with integrated Scope 3 calculation and climate transition plan templates. The platform claims 60% reduction in disclosure preparation time for mid-cap companies.

research.title: Climate Risk and Credit: Integrating Physical Hazards into Bank Lending in South Asia
research.description: Analysis of 12,000 Indian corporate loans found physical climate risk exposure could increase NPAs by 1.8-3.2 percentage points in high-hazard districts by 2035. Recommends granular hazard-adjusted PD models as regulatory best practice.

research.title: Scaling Parametric Insurance in Emerging Markets: India Case Study
research.description: Swiss Re estimates India's parametric insurance market could reach $2.5 billion by 2030, driven by bank-embedded products for agriculture and MSME lending. Current penetration remains below 3% of insurable climate losses.

research.title: Physical Risk Transmission Channels in Indian Banking: An Empirical Assessment
research.description: RBI staff paper analyzing 2015-2025 flood events found a 15-23% increase in 90+ day delinquencies for agricultural and MSME loans in severely affected districts. The paper provides hazard-exposure matrices for 640 districts.

research.title: ESG Data Gaps in Emerging Markets: Implications for Sustainable Finance
research.description: IFC assessment found only 18% of Indian mid-cap companies disclose climate metrics aligned with TCFD, compared to 72% in developed markets. Data gaps are most severe in Scope 3 emissions and physical risk exposure disclosure.

research.title: Climate Scenarios for Financial Risk Assessment in Asia-Pacific
research.description: Technical supplement provides downscaled hazard projections for India across six NGFS scenarios, including state-level agricultural yield shocks and coastal asset exposure. Models are calibrated to 2024-2025 extreme weather observations.

research.title: Natural Catastrophe Risk Pricing in India: 2026 Outlook
research.description: Munich Re projects Indian climate-related insured losses will reach $8-12 billion annually by 2030, with cyclone and flood accounting for 75% of exposure. Report highlights parametric triggers as critical for closing the protection gap.

ourRead.headline: RBI's climate PD/LGD mandate creates an 18-month compliance window where StepChange must win bank mandates before global players localize.
body: This week's signals converge on a single theme: Indian financial regulators are moving from guidance to mandate, compressing timelines and forcing banks to operationalize climate risk faster than global competitors can adapt their products. The RBI's final guidelines, SEBI's BRSR expansion, and IRDAI's parametric sandbox together create a regulatory trifecta that validates StepChange's three strategic bets simultaneously.

The RBI guidelines (policy-1) are the most consequential development. Requiring climate-adjusted PD/LGD reporting quarterly from FY2027-28 means banks have roughly 18 months to build or buy granular physical risk models. The specification for top-50 borrower stress testing creates an immediate procurement trigger. StepChange should position its hazard-to-credit transmission models as RBI-ready, citing the RBI's own staff paper (research-3) showing 15-23% delinquency increases in flood-affected districts as validation of our approach.

The Jupiter-HDFC deal (comp-1) is a warning shot. Jupiter entering India with a marquee private bank validates the market but also signals that global players will pursue large banks directly. However, Jupiter's pricing and implementation timelines typically exceed what Indian banks will tolerate. Sprih's fundraise (comp-2) and MSCI's India-specific module (comp-3) indicate the competitive window is narrowing. StepChange's advantage lies in speed-to-deployment and alignment with RBI's district-level data requirements—neither Jupiter nor MSCI has proprietary Indian hazard data at the granularity the RBI staff paper demands.

The Swiss Re parametric study (research-2) projecting a $2.5 billion Indian market by 2030 should be weaponized in IRDAI sandbox applications. With the regulatory sandbox now open (policy-3), StepChange should prioritize a joint application with a mid-sized insurer and NBFC partner within 60 days. The 45-day payout requirement creates technical barriers that favor firms with real-time hazard monitoring—our data infrastructure advantage.

Watch: Moody's-CRISIL partnership (comp-5) could become the default for PSU banks seeking safety in brand names. Action: Immediately pursue IDBI Bank, Bank of Baroda, and Canara Bank with RBI-aligned pilot proposals before Moody's-CRISIL finalizes pricing. First-mover PSU wins will define market positioning for the next three years.
